attachment.content:
Powered by Entire:
  This conversation will be linked to your next commit.
  1 other active conversation(s) in this workspace will also be included.
  Use 'entire status' for more information.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks to use the Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Managed Agents (`/v1/agents`, `/v1/sessions`); user asks to add, modify, debug, optimize, or improve a Claude feature (prompt caching, cache hit rate, adaptive thinking, compaction, code_execution, batch, files API, citations, memory tool) or a Claude model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; or user asks about prompt caching / cache hit rate / cache reads / cache creation in any project that uses the Anthropic SDK (even without mentioning Claude by name).
DO NOT TRIGGER when: file imports `openai`/non-Anthropic SDK, filename signals another provider (`agent-openai.py`, `*-generic.py`), code is provider-neutral, or task is general programming/ML.
- mk-issue: Use when converting bug complaints media into GitHub issues.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- mk-pr: Create and iterate on a GitHub pull request from an existing GitHub issue using `gh`. Use when given an <issue_number> and a <maintainer_username>, needs to read an agent-friendly issue spec and linked evidence, implement the fix in the local checkout, run issue validation commands and relevant upstream CI-equivalent checks, open a PR linked to the issue in draft mode, request review from the named maintainer by GitHub username/handle, and keep iterating on CI or review feedback until required checks pass or a real blocker is reported.
- agent-ci: Run GitHub Actions CI locally with Agent CI to validate changes before pushing. Use when testing, running checks, or validating code changes.
- mk-shell: Create minimal Nix flake dev shells with only essential runtimes and package managers, using repo-local dependency caches. Use when bootstrapping or updating a devShell.
- gh-fix-ci: Use when a user asks to debug or fix failing GitHub PR checks that run in GitHub Actions; use `gh` to inspect checks and logs, summarize failure context, draft a fix plan, and implement only after explicit approval. Treat external providers (for example Buildkite) as out of scope and report only the details URL.
- yeet: Use only when the user explicitly asks to stage, commit, push, and open a GitHub pull request in one flow using the GitHub CLI (`gh`).
- figma:figma-generate-design: Use this skill alongside figma-use when the task involves translating an application page, view, or multi-section layout into Figma. Triggers: 'write to Figma', 'create in Figma from code', 'push page to Figma', 'take this app/page and build it in Figma', 'create a screen', 'build a landing page in Figma', 'update the Figma screen to match code'. This is the preferred workflow skill whenever the user wants to build or update a full page, screen, or view in Figma from code or a description. Discovers design system components, variables, and styles via search_design_system, imports them, and assembles screens incrementally section-by-section using design system tokens instead of hardcoded values.
- figma:figma-generate-library: Build or update a professional-grade design system in Figma from a codebase. Use when the user wants to create variables/tokens, build component libraries, set up theming (light/dark modes), document foundations, or reconcile gaps between code and Figma. This skill teaches WHAT to build and in WHAT ORDER — it complements the `figma-use` skill which teaches HOW to call the Plugin API. Both skills should be loaded together.
- figma:figma-use: **MANDATORY prerequisite** — you MUST invoke this skill BEFORE every `use_figma` tool call. NEVER call `use_figma` directly without loading this skill first. Skipping it causes common, hard-to-debug failures. Trigger whenever the user wants to perform a write action or a unique read action that requires JavaScript execution in the Figma file context — e.g. create/edit/delete nodes, set up variables or tokens, build components and variants, modify auto-layout or fills, bind variables to properties, or inspect file structure programmatically.
- figma:figma-create-design-system-rules: Generates custom design system rules for the user's codebase. Use when user says "create design system rules", "generate rules for my project", "set up design rules", "customize design system guidelines", or wants to establish project-specific conventions for Figma-to-code workflows. Requires Figma MCP server connection.
- figma:figma-code-connect: Creates and maintains Figma Code Connect template files that map Figma components to code snippets. Use when the user mentions Code Connect, Figma component mapping, design-to-code translation, or asks to create/update .figma.ts or .figma.js files.
- figma:figma-implement-design: Translates Figma designs into production-ready application code with 1:1 visual fidelity. Use when implementing UI code from Figma files, when user mentions "implement design", "generate code", "implement component", provides Figma URLs, or asks to build components matching Figma specs. For Figma canvas writes via `use_figma`, use `figma-use`.

content.text:
Your shareable insights report is ready:
file:///Users/tanishqpalandurkar/.claude/usage-data/report.html

Want to dig into any section or try one of the suggestions?

<ide_opened_file>The user opened the file /Users/tanishqpalandurkar/conductor/workspaces/agent-orchestrator/san-juan/packages/web/src/providers/MuxProvider.tsx in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>

Do you think code quality would improve here and we would benefit from having a consistent state transition management if we use [XState pkg](https://stately.ai/docs/state-machines-and-statecharts) here?

like for eg, take a look at this terminal lifecycle event

content.thinking: The user is asking about whether using XState (a state machine library) would improve code quality in the MuxProvider.tsx file. They want me to look at the terminal lifecycle events. Let me first read the file to understand what's there.

content.text: Let me read the file first before forming an opinion.

content.thinking: Now let me look at the DirectTerminal.tsx and SessionDetail.tsx to understand how the lifecycle events are consumed, to get a full picture.

content.text: Let me also check how `TerminalLifecycleEvent` is consumed downstream.

content.thinking:
Now I have a good picture of the code. Let me analyze:

1. **MuxProvider.tsx** manages WebSocket connection state with a manual `status` string (`"connecting" | "connected" | "reconnecting" | "disconnected"`), refs for tracking state, and an exponential backoff reconnect loop.

2. **TerminalLifecycleEvent** is a simple discriminated union: `opened | exited | error` — consumed in `DirectTerminal.tsx` (to set local error state) and `SessionDetail.tsx` (to track `terminalPhase` — a string enum: `"probing" | "attached" | "exited"`).
   
The real question is whether XState would actually help here. XState could enforce the connection state transitions explicitly—preventing invalid paths like jumping from `disconnected` to `reconnecting`—and would cleanly separate the reconnect logic's side effects from state management using invoked actors. The `terminalPhase` state machine in SessionDetail is also a natural fit for XState's declarative approach, but I need to weigh whether that complexity is worth it for what's currently working with `useState`.

The counterargument is pretty strong though: this connection state machine is genuinely simple—just four states with clear transitions in about 200 lines. Adding XState would introduce boilerplate and a new dependency for something that's already readable and maintainable. The project guidelines explicitly discourage adding dependencies for "improvements" beyond what's asked, and the existing code, while it has some edge cases around async teardown and the `isDestroyedRef` pattern, is fundamentally understandable and functional.

Looking at the actual lifecycle events, `TerminalLifecycleEvent` is really just an event stream with three variants (`opened`, `exited`, `error`), not a state machine itself—the real state machine logic lives in `SessionDetail` with `terminalPhase` managing just three states and a couple of transitions. That's almost trivially simple. Even if I were to model the MuxProvider connection with XState, I'd be defining machines for `connecting`, `connected`, `reconnecting`, and `disconnected` states with actions for WebSocket lifecycle and timer management, but that's arguably more ceremony than the current approach warrants.

The real issue I'm noticing is that `status` and the actual WebSocket state can drift apart because they're managed separately—React state versus refs—and that's a genuine bug XState would naturally prevent by tying the WebSocket instance to state transitions. Also, `TerminalLifecycleEvent` doesn't actually represent the initial idle state, only the events that fire after the terminal opens.

For the connection machine, I'm at the threshold where XState starts making sense—the reconnect timers and guards are exactly what it's designed for. But the terminal lifecycle is genuinely too simple to justify it. The bigger question is whether the session lifecycle in the Node.js core (spawning → working → pr_open and beyond) would benefit from XState, because that's where the complexity actually lives. Adding XState as a dependency just for React would be questionable given the project's constraints anyway.

content.text:
Honest take: **mixed benefit, and it depends which part you're looking at.**

---

**The connection machine (`status` + reconnect logic) — worth considering**

This is where XState would actually pay off. The current code has several implicit state management problems:

1. [MuxProvider.tsx:89](packages/web/src/providers/MuxProvider.tsx#L89) — `isDestroyedRef` is a manual guard that exists purely because async effects outlive their component. XState's actor lifecycle handles this automatically.
2. [MuxProvider.tsx:86-87](packages/web/src/providers/MuxProvider.tsx#L86-L87) — `reconnectAttempt` and `reconnectTimer` are mutable refs that track side-effect state separately from the React `status` string. These can desync — e.g. a reconnect timer fires after `status` is already `"connected"` from a parallel open.
3. The valid transitions are implicit: `connecting → connected`, `connected → reconnecting → connecting`, `connected/reconnecting → disconnected`. Nothing prevents `setStatus("reconnecting")` from being called when already `reconnecting`.

XState would make the invalid transitions impossible and colocate the timer/WS lifecycle with the state it belongs to.

---

**The terminal lifecycle (`TerminalLifecycleEvent`) — not worth it**

This part is straightforward event dispatch, not a state machine. The events flow one-way: `opened → (data...) → exited | error`. The consumer in [SessionDetail.tsx:532-541](packages/web/src/components/SessionDetail.tsx#L532-L541) maps them to a 3-state linear progression (`probing → attached → exited`) with a handful of lines. XState there would be pure overhead.

---

**Practical constraint**

The project has a stated preference against adding new dependencies for marginal gains (the "no UI component libraries" rule reflects this broader philosophy). XState is not a UI library, but it's a non-trivial addition — it would only make sense if you're comfortable owning its configuration patterns across the codebase. Using it for just the connection machine in one provider while everything else is plain React state would feel inconsistent.

**If you do want to improve the connection machine without XState**, the cleaner fix is to move `wsRef`, `reconnectAttempt`, `reconnectTimer`, and `status` into a single reducer with `useReducer`, so all transitions go through one place and can't desync. That gets you 80% of the benefit with zero new dependencies.

lastPrompt: Do you think code quality would improve here and we would benefit from having a consistent state transition management if we use [XState pkg](https://stately.ai/docs/state-machines-and-statecharts) he…
